module.label: Pediatric rash, urticaria, petechiae, or skin infection

use_when: Child or adolescent with rash, hives, urticaria, petechiae, purpura, bruising with rash, cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, skin lesion, wound infection, fever with rash, or prolonged fever with Kawasaki features.

Pediatric rash workup needs child-specific red flags, blanching assessment, anaphylaxis screening, non-blanching rash and meningococcal safety, Kawasaki feature review, and skin infection route decisions.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult rash, dermatology, wound, allergy, infection, or hematology pathway as appropriate.

Unstable child with shock, airway compromise, severe respiratory distress, altered mental status, rapidly progressive purpura, or active anaphylaxis; escalate immediately while documenting focused rash findings.

Primary isolated eye, sore throat, abdominal, respiratory, or urinary complaint without rash or skin findings; use the source-specific pediatric pathway and add this module only if rash changes management.
Known burns, large traumatic wounds, immunocompromised oncology/transplant protocol, or post-operative infection where local specialty pathway overrides this general pediatric rash module.

modifier_add_on_policy.rationale: This validated pediatric rash pack already includes fever/sepsis, anaphylaxis, Kawasaki, petechiae/purpura, and skin-infection routing; generic infection modifier prompts should not be activated by its own rash aliases.

differentialBuckets.label: Non-blanching petechiae or purpura with fever, rapid progression, or unwell appearance
differentialBuckets.action: Treat non-blanching rash with fever or unwell appearance as a senior-review and sepsis/meningococcal-risk problem until serious bacterial infection is judged unlikely.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Serious bacterial infection can present with petechiae or purpura, and the child who is unwell or has fever plus non-blanching rash needs prompt senior review.

source.source_section: key points, definitions, assessment, and management of petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric sepsis and meningococcal disease pathway when the child is unwell.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Meningococcal disease, serious bacterial infection, viral petechiae, mechanical petechiae, hematologic disease, vasculitis, or trauma.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes urgency of senior review, cultures/labs, empiric antibiotics, observation or admission, and whether discharge is allowed.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this broad non-blanching rash frame; guideline-defined danger features and trajectory determine escalation.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Most children with petechiae do not have meningococcal disease, but reassuring appearance does not remove the need for structured assessment.

differentialBuckets.label: Prolonged fever with Kawasaki disease or inflammatory rash features
differentialBuckets.action: Ask for fever duration and Kawasaki features that may have occurred sequentially, especially in infants with prolonged fever and fewer features.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Timely Kawasaki diagnosis and treatment reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk, and infants can have incomplete presentations.

source.source_section: key points, history, complete KD clinical features, and incomplete KD risk
source.implementation_notes: Pair with NICE fever under 5 Kawasaki feature prompts when fever has lasted 5 days or longer.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Kawasaki disease, incomplete Kawasaki disease, MIS-C mimic, viral exanthem, scarlet fever, or other inflammatory syndrome.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes need for senior pediatric review, inflammatory labs, ECG/echocardiography, IVIg/aspirin pathway, and cardiology follow-up.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this Kawasaki frame; diagnosis is clinical and source guidance stresses sequential features and timely treatment.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not rule out Kawasaki disease because all features are not present at the same time.

differentialBuckets.label: Urticaria or angioedema with or without anaphylaxis features
differentialBuckets.action: Separate isolated urticaria from hives or angioedema with airway, breathing, circulation, or gastrointestinal symptoms.
differentialBuckets.rationale: RCH urticaria guidance treats systemic involvement as possible anaphylaxis, while most isolated urticaria does not need admission.

source.source_section: urticaria assessment, anaphylaxis exclusion, consultation, and discharge
source.implementation_notes: Use local anaphylaxis protocol immediately when systemic symptoms are present.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Acute urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis, viral-triggered hives, drug reaction, or chronic urticaria.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes epinephrine/anaphylaxis pathway, observation, antihistamine use, admission, and follow-up.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this urticaria/anaphylaxis frame; systemic involvement changes management regardless of rash appearance.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Isolated hives are common and often viral, but airway, respiratory, cardiovascular, or gastrointestinal symptoms must override low-risk assumptions.

differentialBuckets.label: Cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, or skin and soft tissue infection
differentialBuckets.action: Look for localized warmth, tenderness, fluctuance, drainage, rapidly spreading erythema, immunocompromise, sepsis, or deep/necrotizing infection features.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric SSTI pathways separate outpatient oral therapy from observation/admission, IV antibiotics, drainage, imaging, and urgent surgical or ID advice.

source.source_section: cellulitis and abscess pathway, severe infection criteria, admission and therapy route
source.implementation_notes: Use local antimicrobial guidance and source-control policy.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, necrotizing infection, bite wound infection, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, or sepsis.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes oral versus IV antibiotics, culture/drainage, imaging, admission, surgical consultation, ID advice, and follow-up interval.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this SSTI frame; severity, anatomy, source control, and sepsis features drive management.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This general SSTI frame does not replace bite, orbital, burn, immunocompromised, or post-operative specialty pathways.

differentialBuckets.label: Lower-risk viral exanthem, contact dermatitis, eczema flare, or benign rash pattern
differentialBuckets.action: Classify as lower risk only after non-blanching rash, anaphylaxis, Kawasaki features, severe infection, and systemic danger cues are absent.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Clinician-ready rash support must explicitly show why dangerous pediatric rash syndromes are not being pursued before reassurance or outpatient care.

source.source_section: traffic light risk assessment and disease-specific fever/rash features
source.implementation_notes: Use with pediatric rash morphology and return precautions rather than generic reassurance.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Viral exanthem, contact dermatitis, eczema flare, insect bites, mild urticaria, or other low-acuity rash after red flags are excluded.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Changes outpatient care, symptom treatment, school/daycare guidance, and return precautions while avoiding unnecessary sepsis or antibiotic workup.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not assigned to this lower-risk frame; it depends on absence of source-defined danger features.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not use as reassurance if the rash is rapidly progressive, non-blanching, painful, mucosal, associated with fever, or the child looks unwell.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure and document current temperature with age-appropriate method.
safetyChecks.rationale: Fever changes pediatric rash risk, including meningococcal disease, Kawasaki disease, cellulitis severity, and sepsis routing.
safetyChecks.management_change: Fever moves rash workup toward traffic-light risk assessment, non-blanching rash escalation, Kawasaki duration review, and source testing.
safetyChecks.options: ___ C/F / Febrile / Afebrile / Hypothermic / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Antipyretics and recent measurement timing can mask fever trajectory.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: thermometers and fever risk assessment
source.implementation_notes: Keep temperature in baseline data, not physical exam maneuvers.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and compare with age, fever, pain, hydration, and perfusion context.
safetyChecks.rationale: Tachycardia can signal sepsis, anaphylaxis, dehydration, pain, or severe infection.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal heart rate changes urgency, observation, fluid/anaphylaxis/sepsis pathway, and disposition.
safetyChecks.options: ___ bpm / Normal for age / Tachycardic / Bradycardic / Irregular / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, fever, bronchodilators, pain, and anxiety can change pediatric heart rate.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: traffic light risk assessment and circulation/hydration features
source.implementation_notes: Document trend and age context.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Count respiratory rate for a full interval when feasible and compare with age-adjusted concern.
safetyChecks.rationale: Respiratory distress can accompany anaphylaxis, sepsis, pneumonia with fever/rash, or severe systemic illness.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal respiratory rate changes oxygen support, anaphylaxis/sepsis escalation, and pneumonia or respiratory-source add-on.
safetyChecks.options: ___/min / Normal for age / Tachypneic / Bradypneic / Labored / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Agitation and crying can distort the count; repeat when calm if safe.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data.

source.source_section: traffic light respiratory risk and pneumonia features
source.implementation_notes: Keep as baseline safety data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure oxygen saturation on room air or document oxygen support.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypoxemia changes anaphylaxis, sepsis, pneumonia, and transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.management_change: Low saturation triggers oxygen, respiratory assessment, escalation, and possible admission or transfer.
safetyChecks.options: ___% room air / ___% on oxygen ___ L/min / Hypoxemic / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Borderline pulse oximetry can be inaccurate; confirm signal quality and clinical context.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to oxygen saturation as baseline safety data.

source.source_section: pneumonia and oxygen saturation risk features
source.implementation_notes: Document oxygen device and flow if not room air.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure with a correct-size pediatric cuff when rash is systemic, child is unwell, anaphylaxis is possible, or severe infection is suspected.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypotension is late and dangerous in pediatric shock and can accompany anaphylaxis or sepsis.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal blood pressure changes resuscitation, observation, admission, and transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.options: ___/___ mmHg / Normal for age / Low / High / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Wrong cuff size, crying, and movement can distort pediatric blood pressure.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data.

source.source_section: unwell child signs and shock physiology
source.implementation_notes: Pair with perfusion and mental-status assessment.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status
safetyChecks.action: Document alertness, interaction with caregivers, irritability, lethargy, and ability to wake and stay awake.
safetyChecks.rationale: Altered mental status or inconsolability is a major pediatric rash/fever danger cue.
safetyChecks.management_change: Abnormal mental status triggers urgent senior review, sepsis/meningitis evaluation, and monitored disposition.
safetyChecks.options: Baseline / Irritable / Lethargic / Difficult to rouse / Confused / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Use caregiver baseline because young children may not cooperate with formal testing.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to mental status as baseline acuity data.

source.source_section: traffic light activity and red features
source.implementation_notes: Keep mental status in baseline safety data.

safetyChecks.label: Measure current weight
safetyChecks.action: Measure or confirm current weight for weight-based medication and transfer decisions.
safetyChecks.rationale: Pediatric antibiotics, epinephrine, antihistamines, IVIg, fluids, and analgesia are weight-based.
safetyChecks.management_change: Missing weight blocks safe dosing and handoff for emergency therapy.
safetyChecks.options: ___ kg measured today / Recent reliable weight ___ kg / Estimated only / Not available
safetyChecks.limitations: Estimated weights can be unsafe for high-risk medications; verify when feasible.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to weight as medication-safety data.

source.source_section: weight-based pediatric antimicrobial therapy
source.implementation_notes: Use local dosing guidelines.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask pediatric rash age and vulnerability
requiredQuestions.text: What is the child's exact age band, immunization status, immune risk, prior invasive infection history, and whether the child is under 6 months or an infant under 12 months with prolonged fever?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: Age ___ / Under 6 months / Under 12 months / Immunizations complete / Incomplete immunizations / Immunocompromised / Prior invasive infection / None of these
requiredQuestions.options: Age ___ / Under 6 months / Under 12 months / Immunizations complete / Incomplete immunizations / Immunocompromised / Prior invasive infection / None of these
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask at the start of every pediatric rash workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates infant risk, incomplete immunization, immunocompromise, and Kawasaki incomplete-presentation risk from generic adult rash logic.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive infant, immunization, or immune-risk context lowers the threshold for senior review, sepsis workup, admission, or specialty consultation.
detail_prompts: Document exact age in days/months/years.
Ask whether vaccines are incomplete or unavailable.
Ask about immunosuppression, oncology, transplant, asplenia, biologics, or previous invasive bacterial infection.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to age and vulnerability context; it changes pathway selection and escalation threshold.

source.source_section: history risk factors for petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Use exact age and risk status in handoff.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask rash morphology and progression
requiredQuestions.text: Is the rash petechial, purpuric, non-blanching, urticarial wheals, vesicular, pustular, crusted, cellulitic, abscess-like, target-like, painful, itchy, localized, generalized, rapidly spreading, or changing after pressure?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: Petechiae / Purpura / Non-blanching / Hives-wheals / Vesicles / Pustules-crusting / Cellulitis / Abscess / Target-like / Painful / Itchy / Localized / Generalized / Rapidly spreading / Other ___
requiredQuestions.options: Petechiae / Purpura / Non-blanching / Hives-wheals / Vesicles / Pustules-crusting / Cellulitis / Abscess / Target-like / Painful / Itchy / Localized / Generalized / Rapidly spreading / Other ___
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric rash workup and document positive and negative morphology separately.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Classifies morphology into non-blanching danger rash, urticaria/anaphylaxis, SSTI, viral exanthem, inflammatory rash, or other dermatologic pattern.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Morphology changes urgency, isolation/source control, culture/imaging, senior review, and whether reassurance is safe.
detail_prompts: Ask where rash began and how quickly it spread.
Ask whether pressure or a glass test makes it disappear.
Ask whether pain is out of proportion to appearance.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for broad rash morphology; use it to route source-specific exam and testing.

source.source_section: definitions of petechiae, purpura, blanching, and assessment
source.implementation_notes: Ask morphology in terms caregivers can answer, then verify on exam.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask fever and systemic danger symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Is there fever, rigors, rapid worsening, poor feeding, reduced urine output, lethargy, inconsolable irritability, neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, focal seizure, joint swelling, non-weight bearing, severe pain, vomiting, or breathing difficulty?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: No systemic symptoms / Fever / Rigors / Rapid worsening / Poor feeding / Reduced urine output / Lethargy / Inconsolable irritability / Neck stiffness / Bulging fontanelle / Focal seizure / Joint swelling / Non-weight bearing / Severe pain / Vomiting / Breathing difficulty
requiredQuestions.options: No systemic symptoms / Fever / Rigors / Rapid worsening / Poor feeding / Reduced urine output / Lethargy / Inconsolable irritability / Neck stiffness / Bulging fontanelle / Focal seizure / Joint swelling / Non-weight bearing / Severe pain / Vomiting / Breathing difficulty
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric rash workup because systemic signs change acuity more than rash label alone.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for traffic-light fever danger, meningitis/sepsis, septic arthritis/osteomyelitis, dehydration, and respiratory compromise.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Any danger cue should trigger urgent reassessment, pediatric fever/sepsis pathway, targeted tests, or escalation rather than routine rash care.
detail_prompts: Ask whether the child is acting unlike their usual self.
Ask whether the rash and illness are worsening hour by hour.
Ask about antipyretics and whether fever returns.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this broad systemic danger screen; use guideline red and amber features with vitals and exam.

source.source_section: traffic light risk assessment and disease-specific features
source.implementation_notes: Pair positive danger symptoms with pediatric fever/sepsis intent when needed.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask hives and anaphylaxis symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: If hives, angioedema, or suspected allergy are present, is there tongue or lip swelling, voice change, wheeze, stridor, dyspnea, hypotension, collapse, repetitive vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, known allergen exposure, or epinephrine already given?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: No systemic allergy symptoms / Lip-tongue swelling / Voice change / Wheeze / Stridor / Dyspnea / Hypotension-collapse / Repetitive vomiting / Abdominal pain / Diarrhea / Allergen exposure / Epinephrine already given
requiredQuestions.options: No systemic allergy symptoms / Lip-tongue swelling / Voice change / Wheeze / Stridor / Dyspnea / Hypotension-collapse / Repetitive vomiting / Abdominal pain / Diarrhea / Allergen exposure / Epinephrine already given
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask whenever hives, urticaria, angioedema, allergy, or diffuse itchy rash is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Separates isolated urticaria from possible anaphylaxis with airway, respiratory, cardiovascular, or gastrointestinal involvement.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Systemic features trigger epinephrine/anaphylaxis protocol, observation, and escalation; isolated urticaria can often avoid admission.
detail_prompts: Ask timing from food, medicine, sting, vaccine, or exercise exposure.
Ask whether symptoms are recurring after treatment.
Ask whether an epinephrine autoinjector was used and when.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to anaphylaxis screening; systemic involvement changes emergency management.

source.source_section: urticaria systemic involvement and anaphylaxis suspicion
source.implementation_notes: Do not wait for rash progression when systemic features are present.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask Kawasaki fever and feature history
requiredQuestions.text: Has fever lasted 4 to 5 days or longer, and at any point were there bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, cracked red lips, strawberry tongue, red mouth or throat, swollen red hands or feet, peeling, polymorphous rash, or cervical lymph node swelling?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: Fever duration ___ days / Bilateral conjunctivitis / Cracked red lips / Strawberry tongue / Red mouth-throat / Swollen red hands-feet / Peeling / Polymorphous rash / Cervical lymph node / None of these
requiredQuestions.options: Fever duration ___ days / Bilateral conjunctivitis / Cracked red lips / Strawberry tongue / Red mouth-throat / Swollen red hands-feet / Peeling / Polymorphous rash / Cervical lymph node / None of these
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever lasts 4 days or longer, fever duration is unclear, infant has prolonged unexplained fever, or rash is polymorphous.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies complete or incomplete Kawasaki disease features that may appear sequentially or resolve before assessment.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features trigger senior pediatric review, inflammatory labs, ECG/echocardiography, IVIg/aspirin consideration, and cardiology follow-up.
detail_prompts: Ask parents or carers about features present earlier in the illness.
Ask specifically about infants under 12 months with prolonged fever and fewer features.
Ask whether another diagnosis convincingly explains the fever and rash.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this Kawasaki feature cluster; source guidance uses clinical criteria and sequential history.

source.source_section: history and complete KD clinical features
source.implementation_notes: NICE uses 5 days or longer; RCH allows diagnosis with clinical features and 4 or more days of fever.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask skin infection and source-control history
requiredQuestions.text: Is there a painful red warm area, abscess, drainage, crusting, bite, scratch, penetrating injury, rapidly spreading border, periorbital location, immunocompromise, prior MRSA, recent antibiotics, or failure of oral treatment?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: No SSTI features / Painful red warm area / Abscess / Drainage / Crusting / Bite / Scratch or injury / Rapid spread / Periorbital location / Immunocompromised / Prior MRSA / Recent antibiotics / Failed oral treatment
requiredQuestions.options: No SSTI features / Painful red warm area / Abscess / Drainage / Crusting / Bite / Scratch or injury / Rapid spread / Periorbital location / Immunocompromised / Prior MRSA / Recent antibiotics / Failed oral treatment
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when rash is focal, painful, warm, draining, crusted, wound-related, or cellulitis/abscess is possible.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, bite wound infection, periorbital/orbital cellulitis, necrotizing infection, or treatment failure.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive features change need for drainage, culture, imaging, oral versus IV antibiotics, surgical/ID advice, and admission.
detail_prompts: Document whether erythema is spreading and at what pace.
Ask about fever and systemic symptoms paired with focal skin infection.
Ask about anatomic location: face, orbit, hand, genital/perineal, joint-adjacent, or device site.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for broad SSTI source history; severity and anatomy determine management.

source.source_section: cellulitis and abscess pathway and severe infection criteria
source.implementation_notes: Use local antimicrobial stewardship and surgical drainage pathways.

requiredQuestions.label: Ask bleeding trauma and vasculitis clues
requiredQuestions.text: With petechiae, purpura, or bruising, is there mucosal bleeding, nosebleeds, gum bleeding, abdominal pain, joint pain or swelling, difficulty mobilizing, hematuria, recent vomiting/coughing, tight pressure exposure, injury, safeguarding concern, or travel/sick contacts?
requiredQuestions.answer_options: No bleeding or vasculitis clues / Mucosal bleeding / Nosebleeds / Gum bleeding / Abdominal pain / Joint pain-swelling / Difficulty mobilizing / Hematuria / Vomiting-coughing / Pressure exposure / Injury / Safeguarding concern / Travel / Sick contacts
requiredQuestions.options: No bleeding or vasculitis clues / Mucosal bleeding / Nosebleeds / Gum bleeding / Abdominal pain / Joint pain-swelling / Difficulty mobilizing / Hematuria / Vomiting-coughing / Pressure exposure / Injury / Safeguarding concern / Travel / Sick contacts
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when petechiae, purpura, bruising, or non-blanching rash is present.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for mechanical petechiae, trauma, child abuse, ITP, leukemia, DIC, HSP/IgA vasculitis, HUS, and imported infection context.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive findings change CBC/film/coagulation/urinalysis threshold, safeguarding review, hematology/pediatric consultation, and discharge safety.
detail_prompts: Ask whether spots are limited above the nipple line after vomiting or coughing.
Ask whether bruising pattern fits the history.
Ask about abdominal pain and hematuria with lower-limb purpura.
requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for bleeding/vasculitis screening; it routes guideline-defined tests and consultation.

source.source_section: history and differential diagnoses for petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Do not classify as benign until serious causes and safeguarding concerns are considered.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask chronic urticaria duration and systemic features
conditionalQuestions.text: Have hives or angioedema persisted or recurred for more than 6 weeks, and are there fever, bruising, joint pain, weight loss, abdominal pain, or other systemic features?
conditionalQuestions.answer_options: Less than 6 weeks / More than 6 weeks / Fever / Bruising / Joint pain / Weight loss / Abdominal pain / Other systemic features / None
conditionalQuestions.options: Less than 6 weeks / More than 6 weeks / Fever / Bruising / Joint pain / Weight loss / Abdominal pain / Other systemic features / None
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when hives are recurrent, chronic, or associated with non-allergic systemic symptoms.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies chronic urticaria or urticarial vasculitis/systemic disease features requiring pediatric or allergy advice.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive chronic/systemic features change follow-up, antihistamine escalation, specialist advice, and need to avoid simple viral-hives reassurance.
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to chronic urticaria routing; duration and systemic features change referral and follow-up.

source.source_section: chronic urticaria and consultation criteria
source.implementation_notes: Ask after anaphylaxis has been excluded.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask adolescent pregnancy and medication context
conditionalQuestions.text: For an adolescent, is pregnancy possible or present, postpartum state possible, or are there new medicines, antibiotics, anticonvulsants, isotretinoin, NSAIDs, sexual exposure, or genital lesions?
conditionalQuestions.answer_options: Pregnancy possible / Currently pregnant / Postpartum / New antibiotic / Anticonvulsant / Isotretinoin / NSAID / Sexual exposure / Genital lesions / None of these
conditionalQuestions.options: Pregnancy possible / Currently pregnant / Postpartum / New antibiotic / Anticonvulsant / Isotretinoin / NSAID / Sexual exposure / Genital lesions / None of these
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask confidentially when an adolescent has generalized rash, mucosal findings, genital rash, medication exposure, or treatment decisions affected by pregnancy.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies pregnancy-sensitive medication/testing issues, STI or genital rash mimics, and medication-associated severe rash concern.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive context changes pregnancy testing, medication safety, STI testing, confidential care, and specialty routing.
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to adolescent pregnancy/medication context; it changes safety and testing.

source.source_section: risk assessment and imported infections; adolescent safety context added for medication/testing governance
source.implementation_notes: Use local adolescent consent, safeguarding, and medication safety policies.

conditionalQuestions.label: Ask travel outbreak and contact exposure
conditionalQuestions.text: Was there recent travel, measles/varicella exposure, under-immunized contact, daycare/school outbreak, tick or animal exposure, food or water exposure, or household sick contacts?
conditionalQuestions.answer_options: Travel / Measles exposure / Varicella exposure / Under-immunized contact / School-daycare outbreak / Tick-animal exposure / Food-water exposure / Household sick contacts / None
conditionalQuestions.options: Travel / Measles exposure / Varicella exposure / Under-immunized contact / School-daycare outbreak / Tick-animal exposure / Food-water exposure / Household sick contacts / None
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever with rash, generalized exanthem, incomplete immunization, or outbreak concern is present.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Routes infection-control, public-health, imported infection, and isolation decisions without making a generic rash recommendation authoritative.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Positive exposure changes isolation, testing, public-health notification, source-specific workup, and return-to-school guidance.
conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for broad exposure history; use it to route source-specific testing and public-health action.

source.source_section: imported infections and fever assessment
source.implementation_notes: Public-health reporting depends on local jurisdiction and suspected disease.

requiredExam.label: Inspect rash morphology and distribution
requiredExam.technique: Expose enough skin with consent and warmth preserved; inspect lesion type, color, size, distribution, symmetry, trunk/extremity/face involvement, and whether lesions cluster above or below the nipple line.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric rash workup.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Rash morphology and distribution: non-blanching rash, urticaria, viral exanthem, cellulitis/abscess, Kawasaki pattern, mechanical petechiae, or safeguarding concern.
requiredExam.management_change: Morphology changes need for blanching test, senior review, isolation, infection workup, allergy pathway, source control, or outpatient reassurance.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; rash morphology routes guideline-specific next steps.
requiredExam.limitations: Rash appearance varies by skin tone, lighting, pressure, and timing; document uncertainty and photographs only per local policy.

source.source_section: definitions and assessment of petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Document whether lesions are petechiae, purpura, wheals, vesicles, pustules, or cellulitic.

requiredExam.label: Test rash blanching with clear pressure
requiredExam.technique: Press a clear glass or transparent object firmly over representative lesions and observe whether redness disappears while maintaining child comfort.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when petechiae, purpura, fever with rash, or non-blanching concern is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Non-blanching petechiae/purpura versus blanching exanthem or urticaria.
requiredExam.management_change: Non-blanching lesions with fever or unwell appearance trigger senior review, sepsis/meningococcal assessment, and discharge restraint.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for the glass test; use it to classify non-blanching rash and apply guideline escalation.
requiredExam.equipment_needed: clear glass or transparent pressure tool
requiredExam.limitations: Very dark lesions, bruising, poor lighting, and child movement can make blanching uncertain.

source.source_section: glass test and non-blanching rash definitions
source.implementation_notes: A non-blanching result is a routing finding, not a stand-alone diagnosis.

requiredExam.label: Inspect oral mucosa and lips
requiredExam.technique: With consent and appropriate light, inspect lips, tongue, oral mucosa, and pharynx for erythema, fissuring, strawberry tongue, exudate, ulceration, swelling, or dehydration.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, Kawasaki concern, urticaria/angioedema, mucosal symptoms, or systemic rash is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Kawasaki mucosal changes, angioedema, dehydration, pharyngitis/scarlet fever mimic, or severe mucosal rash concern.
requiredExam.management_change: Findings change Kawasaki evaluation, anaphylaxis escalation, hydration support, infectious differential, and specialty review.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; mucosal findings are interpreted in source-defined Kawasaki, anaphylaxis, and infection contexts.
requiredExam.limitations: Young or distressed children may not tolerate a full oral exam; avoid force and document limits.

source.source_section: mucosal changes in complete Kawasaki disease
source.implementation_notes: Document whether mucosa is exudative or ulcerated because that may argue against typical Kawasaki disease.

requiredExam.label: Inspect conjunctival injection
requiredExam.technique: Inspect both eyes without fluorescein unless separately indicated; note conjunctival injection, exudate, pain, photophobia, or periorbital swelling.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever with rash, Kawasaki concern, eye symptoms, or periorbital skin infection is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Kawasaki conjunctival injection, infectious conjunctivitis mimic, orbital/periorbital cellulitis clue, or eye emergency.
requiredExam.management_change: Findings change Kawasaki workup, eye/HEENT add-on, imaging/antibiotics for periorbital infection, or urgent ophthalmology/ED routing.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; conjunctival findings are interpreted with fever duration and other features.
requiredExam.limitations: Crying, allergies, and infection can cause redness; use with the full syndrome.

source.source_section: conjunctival injection in complete Kawasaki disease
source.implementation_notes: Pain or photophobia should trigger an eye-specific workup.

requiredExam.label: Inspect skin infection border warmth and drainage
requiredExam.technique: Inspect the suspected infected area for erythema border, warmth, tenderness cues, fluctuance, drainage, crusting, necrosis, crepitus, streaking, and high-risk location.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, wound infection, bite, or focal painful rash is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Cellulitis, abscess, impetigo, necrotizing infection, lymphangitis, bite wound infection, or high-risk anatomic SSTI.
requiredExam.management_change: Findings change drainage/culture need, oral versus IV antibiotics, imaging, admission, and surgical/ID consultation.
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; source control and severity determine SSTI management.
requiredExam.equipment_needed: gloves and skin marker if marking border per local practice
requiredExam.limitations: Deep infection can have subtle skin findings; pain out of proportion or toxicity should escalate even if inspection is limited.

source.source_section: SSTI pathway severity and source control criteria
source.implementation_notes: Avoid squeezing abscesses during diagnostic inspection unless performing a procedure.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate cervical lymph node size
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate anterior cervical nodes gently and document laterality, tenderness, and whether any node is approximately 1.5 cm or larger.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Kawasaki disease, throat infection, lymphadenitis, or prolonged fever with rash is possible.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Kawasaki cervical adenopathy, lymphadenitis, malignancy/hematologic clue, or infection source.
conditionalExam.management_change: Findings change Kawasaki criteria count, antibiotic/source evaluation, and pediatric consultation threshold.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; node findings are interpreted with fever duration and other features.
conditionalExam.limitations: Reactive nodes are common; do not use node size alone to diagnose Kawasaki disease or malignancy.

source.source_section: cervical adenopathy in complete Kawasaki disease
source.implementation_notes: Use as one Kawasaki feature, not a stand-alone diagnosis.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate liver edge and spleen tip
conditionalExam.technique: Palpate gently for liver edge and spleen tip with the child supine when tolerated; stop if pain or distress is significant.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when petechiae, purpura, bruising, pallor, lymphadenopathy, abdominal pain, or hematologic disease is possible.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Hepatosplenomegaly suggesting hematologic disease, systemic infection, HSP/vasculitis complication, or alternate diagnosis.
conditionalExam.management_change: Findings change CBC/film/hemolysis/coagulation testing, hematology/pediatric consultation, and discharge safety.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; hepatosplenomegaly changes hematology and systemic illness routing.
conditionalExam.limitations: Body habitus, guarding, and cooperation can limit accuracy; abnormal concern should be confirmed clinically.

source.source_section: examination for hematological causes in petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Pair with lymph node and bleeding history when hematologic disease is possible.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect palms soles and perineum
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect palms, soles, fingers, toes, and perineal area when clinically appropriate and consented, looking for erythema, edema, desquamation, polymorphous rash, or painful swelling.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when Kawasaki disease, hand-foot-mouth mimic, HSP/vasculitis, or widespread febrile rash is possible.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Kawasaki extremity changes, perineal rash/desquamation, hand-foot-mouth mimic, vasculitis, or other inflammatory rash.
conditionalExam.management_change: Findings change Kawasaki criteria count, infectious differential, cardiology/echo route, and follow-up.
conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available; extremity findings are interpreted with fever duration and other Kawasaki features.
conditionalExam.limitations: Perineal inspection requires privacy, consent, chaperone practice, and only when clinically indicated.

source.source_section: rash and extremity changes in Kawasaki disease
source.implementation_notes: Do not perform perineal inspection unless it changes the differential or safeguarding/consent context is clear.

redFlags.label: Non-blanching rash with fever or unwell child
redFlags.action: Obtain urgent senior pediatric review and use sepsis/meningococcal pathway when a child has fever or unwell appearance with petechiae/purpura or rapidly progressive non-blanching rash.
redFlags.rationale: Serious bacterial infection can present with non-blanching rash, and RCH recommends prompt senior review for fever with petechiae/purpura.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Meningococcal disease, sepsis, serious bacterial infection, DIC, or other dangerous non-blanching rash cause.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers urgent assessment, cultures/labs, empiric antibiotics when indicated, admission/transfer decision, and discharge restraint.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this red flag; guideline danger features determine urgent escalation.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of fever at one time point does not exclude serious illness if antipyretics or evolving disease are present.

source.source_section: assessment and management of fever with petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Use local antimicrobial and sepsis protocols.

redFlags.label: Urticaria with airway breathing circulation or GI symptoms
redFlags.action: Treat hives/angioedema with airway, respiratory, cardiovascular, collapse, or repetitive gastrointestinal symptoms as possible anaphylaxis.
redFlags.rationale: RCH urticaria guidance states anaphylaxis should be suspected when urticaria involves other systems.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Anaphylaxis, angioedema, airway compromise, bronchospasm, shock, or severe allergic reaction.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers epinephrine/anaphylaxis protocol, airway/oxygen/IV access consideration, observation, and escalation.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to anaphylaxis escalation; systemic involvement changes emergency management.
redFlags.limitations: Rash may be absent in anaphylaxis; do not delay treatment waiting for skin findings.

source.source_section: systemic involvement and anaphylaxis consultation/discharge criteria
source.implementation_notes: Use local anaphylaxis policy and weight-based epinephrine.

redFlags.label: Prolonged fever with Kawasaki features
redFlags.action: Escalate for senior pediatric review when fever lasts 4 to 5 days or longer with Kawasaki features, especially in infants with incomplete presentation.
redFlags.rationale: Timely IVIg under senior guidance reduces coronary aneurysm risk, and infants can have fewer clinical features.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Complete or incomplete Kawasaki disease with coronary artery aneurysm risk.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers inflammatory labs, ECG/echo/cardiology route, IVIg/aspirin consideration, and follow-up.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this red flag; Kawasaki diagnosis uses clinical criteria and source-defined treatment urgency.
redFlags.limitations: Features can appear sequentially and do not need to be simultaneous.

source.source_section: key points and clinical features
source.implementation_notes: NICE under-5 guidance uses fever 5 days or longer; RCH notes diagnosis can be made with clinical features and 4 or more days of fever.

redFlags.label: Severe skin infection or necrotizing features
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently for IV antibiotics, source control, surgical/ID advice, or admission when skin infection is paired with immunocompromise, hemodynamic instability, sepsis, deep infection, necrosis, crepitus, or pain out of proportion.
redFlags.rationale: Pediatric SSTI pathway severe criteria include immunocompromise, signs of deep/necrotizing infection, hemodynamic instability, and sepsis.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: Severe cellulitis, abscess with systemic illness, necrotizing soft-tissue infection, bacteremia, or high-risk site infection.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers admission, IV therapy, imaging/procedure planning, surgical/ID consultation, and transfer consideration.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to severe SSTI red flags; source-defined severity criteria determine route.
redFlags.limitations: Early necrotizing infection can have subtle skin changes; pain, toxicity, and rapid spread should override a mild-looking rash.

source.source_section: severe infection criteria and inpatient route
source.implementation_notes: Use local surgical, ID, and antimicrobial protocols.

redFlags.label: Purpura with bleeding abdominal joint or hematology features
redFlags.action: Do not discharge as simple rash when petechiae/purpura are paired with mucosal bleeding, pallor, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, abdominal pain, hematuria, joint swelling, difficulty mobilizing, or safeguarding concern.
redFlags.rationale: RCH petechiae/purpura guidance lists hematologic, vasculitic, traumatic, and non-accidental injury causes that need review.
redFlags.diagnostic_target: ITP, leukemia, DIC, HSP/IgA vasculitis, HUS, trauma, child abuse, or systemic infection.
redFlags.management_change: Triggers CBC/film/coagulation/urinalysis, pediatric/hematology/safeguarding review, observation, and follow-up rather than reassurance.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to this red flag; associated features change testing and consultation.
redFlags.limitations: Mechanical petechiae may be benign, but distribution and history must fit before reassurance.

source.source_section: differential diagnoses and examination for hematologic causes
source.implementation_notes: Escalate safeguarding concerns according to local policy.

initialTests.label: Non-blanching rash senior-review tests
initialTests.action: For fever or unwell child with petechiae/purpura, use senior pediatric review to decide CBC with film, blood culture, inflammatory markers, coagulation studies, glucose/lactate or sepsis labs, and empiric antibiotics without delaying urgent treatment.
initialTests.rationale: Non-blanching rash with fever or unwell appearance can signal serious bacterial infection; testing and antibiotics depend on clinical risk and local pathway.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Serious bacterial infection, meningococcal disease, DIC, hematologic disease, or low-risk non-blanching rash after assessment.
initialTests.management_change: Results and clinical status change antibiotics, admission/transfer, observation duration, hematology review, and discharge safety.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Guidelines do not provide one universal LR for non-blanching rash tests; testing is selected by illness severity, rash features, and senior review.
initialTests.limitations: Do not delay sepsis treatment in an unstable child while awaiting labs.

source.source_section: management and flowchart principles for petechiae/purpura
source.implementation_notes: Local flowchart determines exact test bundle.

initialTests.label: Kawasaki labs ECG and echocardiography route
initialTests.action: When Kawasaki disease is possible, obtain senior pediatric review for inflammatory labs, CBC, electrolytes/liver tests/albumin as locally used, urinalysis when helpful, ECG, echocardiography, and cardiology follow-up planning.
initialTests.rationale: Kawasaki has no single diagnostic test; timely evaluation is needed because untreated disease can cause coronary artery aneurysms.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Complete or incomplete Kawasaki disease and coronary artery involvement.
initialTests.management_change: Testing changes IVIg/aspirin/corticosteroid decisions, cardiology involvement, follow-up echo schedule, and admission.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to the Kawasaki testing route; tests support a clinical diagnosis and cardiac risk management.
initialTests.limitations: Normal early labs or incomplete features do not independently exclude Kawasaki disease in infants or evolving illness.

source.source_section: diagnosis, treatment urgency, and coronary artery aneurysm risk
source.implementation_notes: Follow local Kawasaki and cardiology pathway for exact lab and echo timing.

initialTests.label: SSTI culture imaging and source control route
initialTests.action: For suspected abscess or severe SSTI, decide whether incision/drainage, culture of purulent drainage, ultrasound for abscess, imaging for deep infection, or surgical/ID consultation is needed.
initialTests.rationale: Skin infection management changes when there is purulence, abscess, deep infection, necrotizing concern, high-risk anatomy, or sepsis.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Drainable abscess, cellulitis, necrotizing infection, orbital/deep infection, resistant organism, or source-control need.
initialTests.management_change: Findings change source control, oral versus IV therapy, admission, antimicrobial selection, and specialist consultation.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to this SSTI test bundle; imaging/culture are selected by abscess, severity, anatomy, and treatment route.
initialTests.limitations: Do not culture intact cellulitis without drainage unless local pathway indicates; do not delay surgical review for necrotizing concern.

source.source_section: skin and soft tissue infection pathway and special clinical scenarios
source.implementation_notes: Use local procedure, ultrasound, and antimicrobial guidance.

initialTests.label: Anaphylaxis immediate treatment route
initialTests.action: If urticaria or angioedema involves airway, respiratory, cardiovascular, or repetitive gastrointestinal symptoms, activate local anaphylaxis protocol with weight-based epinephrine and observation/escalation.
initialTests.rationale: Systemic symptoms with urticaria can represent anaphylaxis and require emergency treatment rather than rash testing.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: Anaphylaxis, airway compromise, bronchospasm, hypotension, collapse, or recurrent systemic allergic reaction.
initialTests.management_change: Changes immediate epinephrine use, airway/oxygen/IV access, monitoring, discharge criteria, and allergy follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to immediate anaphylaxis action; treatment is triggered by systemic involvement.
initialTests.limitations: Do not use antihistamines alone for systemic anaphylaxis features.

source.source_section: systemic involvement and anaphylaxis consultation/discharge criteria
source.implementation_notes: Use local anaphylaxis guideline for exact epinephrine dose and observation period.

initialTests.label: Bleeding vasculitis and hematology screen
initialTests.action: When petechiae/purpura are paired with bleeding, pallor, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, abdominal pain, joint symptoms, hematuria, or safeguarding concern, add CBC with film, coagulation studies, urinalysis, renal function, blood pressure review, and pediatric/hematology/safeguarding consultation as indicated.
initialTests.rationale: RCH lists hematologic, vasculitic, HUS, DIC, trauma, and non-accidental injury causes that require targeted assessment.
initialTests.diagnostic_target: ITP, leukemia, DIC, HSP/IgA vasculitis, HUS, traumatic bruising, child abuse, or systemic infection.
initialTests.management_change: Results change discharge safety, observation/admission, hematology/nephrology/pediatric review, safeguarding escalation, and follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not attached to this broad hematology/vasculitis screen; tests are selected because associated findings change management.
initialTests.limitations: Testing should be guided by clinical findings and senior review, not every low-risk localized petechia.

source.source_section: differential diagnoses and examination for hematological causes
source.implementation_notes: Follow local HSP/IgA vasculitis and child-protection guidance when suggested.

dispositionRules.label: Immediate sepsis or meningococcal route
dispositionRules.action: If the child is unwell, has fever with petechiae/purpura, rapidly progressive non-blanching rash, shock, altered mental status, or meningitis features, escalate to senior pediatric/sepsis pathway and do not discharge.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pediatric non-blanching rash with fever or illness can represent serious bacterial infection and needs prompt review.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Meningococcal disease, sepsis, meningitis, DIC, or other life-threatening rash presentation.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes urgency, antibiotics, labs/cultures, admission/transfer, and handoff priority.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to disposition escalation; guideline danger features determine safety.
dispositionRules.limitations: Escalation should not wait for full rash classification when the child is unstable.

source.source_section: management and consultation/transfer criteria
source.implementation_notes: Use local retrieval and PICU/neonatal ICU pathway when needed.

dispositionRules.label: Anaphylaxis observation and discharge route
dispositionRules.action: For urticaria, discharge only after anaphylaxis has been excluded or treated and observation/safety-net criteria are met; arrange allergy follow-up when indicated.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH notes most urticaria does not need admission, but discharge requires anaphylaxis exclusion.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Isolated urticaria versus anaphylaxis, angioedema, or recurrent systemic allergic reaction.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes observation, epinephrine prescription/training, trigger avoidance, allergy referral, and discharge safety.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to urticaria disposition; systemic involvement and observation determine route.
dispositionRules.limitations: Recurrent symptoms, airway involvement, or caregiver inability to return should lower discharge threshold.

source.source_section: consider discharge and consultation criteria
source.implementation_notes: Use local anaphylaxis discharge rules.

dispositionRules.label: Kawasaki senior review and cardiac route
dispositionRules.action: When Kawasaki disease is possible, arrange senior pediatric review, cardiac evaluation pathway, treatment decision, and follow-up rather than routine rash discharge.
dispositionRules.rationale: RCH emphasizes timely diagnosis and IVIg under senior guidance to reduce coronary aneurysm risk.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Complete or incomplete Kawasaki disease and coronary artery aneurysm risk.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes admission, IVIg/aspirin/corticosteroid consideration, echocardiography, cardiology follow-up, and return precautions.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to Kawasaki disposition; clinical criteria and cardiac risk determine route.
dispositionRules.limitations: Incomplete Kawasaki can occur in infants; do not require all features before senior review.

source.source_section: key points and timely treatment
source.implementation_notes: Follow local Kawasaki treatment protocol and cardiology follow-up schedule.

dispositionRules.label: SSTI outpatient observation or admission route
dispositionRules.action: Stable mild-to-moderate cellulitis/impetigo can use local oral therapy and close follow-up; severe infection, immunocompromise, sepsis, deep/necrotizing concern, or failed outpatient therapy requires observation/admission or transfer.
dispositionRules.rationale: The pediatric SSTI pathway separates outpatient therapy, observation, and inpatient admission by severity and host risk.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Mild/moderate SSTI, severe SSTI, abscess needing source control, or deep infection.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes oral versus IV therapy, drainage/imaging, observation/admission, follow-up interval, and consultation.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to SSTI disposition; severity and host factors determine route.
dispositionRules.limitations: Local antimicrobial susceptibility and high-risk locations can override the general route.

source.source_section: outpatient, observation, and inpatient criteria for SSTI
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric antimicrobial and follow-up criteria.

dispositionRules.label: Low-risk rash discharge and return precautions
dispositionRules.action: Use outpatient care only when the child is well appearing, vitals are acceptable, anaphylaxis/non-blanching rash/Kawasaki/severe SSTI/systemic danger cues have been excluded, and caregivers have clear return precautions.
dispositionRules.rationale: Low-risk rash adoption depends on showing why dangerous pediatric rash syndromes were not pursued and what should trigger reassessment.
dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Low-risk viral exanthem, contact dermatitis, eczema flare, mild urticaria, or localized benign rash.
dispositionRules.management_change: Changes discharge counseling, symptom care, follow-up timing, and return-for-review triggers.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to safety-net instructions; discharge depends on absence of red flags and reliable reassessment.
dispositionRules.limitations: Rash can evolve; caregivers should return for fever, non-blanching lesions, rapid spread, breathing symptoms, swelling, lethargy, poor intake, or worsening pain.

source.source_section: traffic light risk assessment and safety-netting for feverish illness
source.implementation_notes: Tailor safety-netting to age, rash pattern, fever, and access to reassessment.

root.description: Activate for child or adolescent with rash, petechiae, purpura, non-blanching lesions, hives, angioedema, suspected anaphylaxis, prolonged fever with rash, cellulitis, impetigo, abscess, or clinician-chosen pediatric skin evaluation.

endpoint_missing_context.description: Use when pediatric rash cannot be safely classified because core morphology, fever, acuity, medication, immune-risk, or follow-up data are unavailable.

pediatric_rash_skin_initial_assessment_b.description: Initial pediatric rash assessment must collect the data needed to identify sepsis/meningococcemia, Kawasaki disease, anaphylaxis, urticaria, SSTI, hematology/vasculitis mimics, and safe disposition.

criteria_options.label: Kawasaki disease: fever >=4 days plus at least 4 principal clinical features; cervical node criterion >=1.5 cm
criteria_options.criteria_text: Complete Kawasaki disease can be diagnosed with fever >=4 days plus at least 4 principal features at any point in illness and no better alternative cause; cervical adenopathy criterion is a cluster of anterior cervical nodes >=1.5 cm.
criteria_options.source_section: Clinical features of complete Kawasaki disease

criteria_options.label: Kawasaki echo/high-risk cutoffs: coronary aneurysm Z-score >=2.5; EF <55%; fractional shortening <28%; coronary dilation Z-score 2 to 2.5
criteria_options.criteria_text: Incomplete Kawasaki support includes coronary aneurysm Z-score >=2.5 or at least 3 echo features; high-risk includes age <=6 months, right CA/LAD Z-score >=2.5, coronary Z-score >=2, IVIG delay >=10 days, IVIG resistance fever >=38 C at >=36 hours, platelets >=450 x10^9/L, or CRP >=130 mg/L.
criteria_options.source_section: Incomplete Kawasaki diagnosis and risk stratification

criteria_options.label: Kawasaki treatment: IVIG 2 g/kg over 8-12 hours plus aspirin 5 mg/kg daily until cardiology review around 6 weeks
criteria_options.criteria_text: Primary Kawasaki therapy is IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 8-12 hours and aspirin 5 mg/kg orally daily until follow-up cardiology review around 6 weeks post onset.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment

criteria_options.label: Non-blanching rash risk: fever plus ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, CRT >=3 seconds, neck stiffness, shock, altered mental status, or difficulty mobilizing
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE flags meningococcal disease when fever and non-blanching rash occur, especially with ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, or neck stiffness; RCH treats unwell children with petechiae/purpura as urgent and notes shock, altered conscious state, abnormal vitals, and limb tenderness or difficulty mobilizing as unwell features.
criteria_options.source_section: Meningococcal disease signs and petechiae/purpura assessment

criteria_options.label: Unwell petechiae/purpura antibiotics: ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily max 4 g, avoid neonates <41 weeks; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV by neonatal age interval
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH petechiae/purpura antimicrobial notes list ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily (maximum 4 g) and advise avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates <41 weeks gestation, especially if jaundiced or receiving calcium-containing solutions; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV is dosed every 12 hours in week 1, every 6-8 hours in weeks 2-4, and every 6 hours after week 4.
criteria_options.source_section: Management antibiotic dosing footnote

criteria_options.label: Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline 1:1000 by weight band, repeat/escalate after 2 doses; observe >=4 hours after last adrenaline or symptom onset
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH anaphylaxis guidance uses IM adrenaline 1:1000 volumes by weight band (0.1 mL under 7.5-10 kg, 0.15 mL at 15 kg, 0.2 mL at 20 kg, 0.3 mL at 30 kg, 0.4 mL at 40 kg, 0.5 mL above 50 kg), defines refractory anaphylaxis as persisting respiratory or cardiovascular symptoms despite 2 appropriate IM doses, and observes all children for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose or symptom onset.
criteria_options.source_section: Adrenaline dose chart, observation and admission, urticaria anaphylaxis screen

criteria_options.label: Urticaria: acute hives use cetirizine age dosing; severe nonresponse prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg max 60 mg; chronic >6 weeks may increase cetirizine to 4x dose
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH urticaria guidance states acute urticaria usually needs no investigations, uses non-sedating antihistamines such as cetirizine with age-based dosing, may use one oral prednisolone dose 0.5-1 mg/kg (max 60 mg) in severe cases not responding to antihistamines, and chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) can increase cetirizine up to 4 times the recommended daily dose with specialist advice if symptoms persist.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment, chronic urticaria, consultation and discharge

criteria_options.label: Pediatric SSTI: abscess/cellulitis <5 cm mild, >5 cm or systemic symptoms moderate; oral antibiotics 5 days; TMP-SMX, doxycycline, clindamycin, cephalexin doses
criteria_options.criteria_text: Johns Hopkins All Children's SSTI pathway classifies suppurative abscess/cellulitis as mild when total cellulitis plus abscess is <5 cm with no systemic symptoms, moderate when >5 cm and/or systemic symptoms, and severe/complicated with hemodynamic instability, toxic appearance, immunocompromise, necrotizing concern, poor adherence/social concern, failed I&D, or failed outpatient therapy. It recommends I&D as primary abscess treatment, oral antibiotics for 5 days, and lists TMP-SMX 8 mg/kg TMP component q12h max 320 mg TMP, doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg q12h max 100 mg, clindamycin 10 mg/kg q8h max 450 mg, or cephalexin 50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h max 500 mg.
criteria_options.source_section: Non-suppurative and suppurative SSTI algorithms and antimicrobial therapy

criteria_options.label: Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline 1:1000 by weight band, repeat/escalate after 2 doses; observe >=4 hours after last adrenaline or symptom onset
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH anaphylaxis guidance uses IM adrenaline 1:1000 volumes by weight band (0.1 mL under 7.5-10 kg, 0.15 mL at 15 kg, 0.2 mL at 20 kg, 0.3 mL at 30 kg, 0.4 mL at 40 kg, 0.5 mL above 50 kg), defines refractory anaphylaxis as persisting respiratory or cardiovascular symptoms despite 2 appropriate IM doses, and observes all children for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose or symptom onset.

criteria_options.label: Pediatric SSTI: abscess/cellulitis <5 cm mild, >5 cm or systemic symptoms moderate; oral antibiotics 5 days; TMP-SMX, doxycycline, clindamycin, cephalexin doses

children.label: Non-blanching rash emergency
children.edgeLabel: Fever or unwell child plus petechiae/purpura, purpura >2 mm, CRT >=3 sec, neck stiffness, shock, altered mental status, limb tenderness, age <6 months, incomplete immunization, or immunocompromise

criteria.description: Use when non-blanching rash features or systemic illness make meningococcal disease, sepsis, DIC, leukemia, HUS, or another urgent cause possible.

criteria_options.label: Non-blanching rash risk: fever plus ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, CRT >=3 seconds, neck stiffness, shock, altered mental status, or difficulty mobilizing
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE flags meningococcal disease when fever and non-blanching rash occur, especially with ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, or neck stiffness; RCH treats unwell children with petechiae/purpura as urgent and notes shock, altered conscious state, abnormal vitals, and limb tenderness or difficulty mobilizing as unwell features.

criteria_options.label: Unwell petechiae/purpura antibiotics: ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily max 4 g, avoid neonates <41 weeks; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV by neonatal age interval
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH petechiae/purpura antimicrobial notes list ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily (maximum 4 g) and advise avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates <41 weeks gestation, especially if jaundiced or receiving calcium-containing solutions; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV is dosed every 12 hours in week 1, every 6-8 hours in weeks 2-4, and every 6 hours after week 4.

children.action: Get senior pediatric review immediately. Assess ABCs, perfusion, mental status, CRT, rash distribution and purpura size, meningitis signs, bleeding, hepatosplenomegaly, lymph nodes, joints, and mobility. Use sepsis/meningococcal care when unwell; obtain culture/FBE-film/coagulation/CRP per senior plan; give ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily max 4 g unless neonatal contraindication applies, or cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV by neonatal age interval; admit or transfer if care exceeds local capability.

children.action: Consult senior pediatrics and cardiology. Order FBE, CRP, ESR, UEC, LFTs, blood culture, urine microscopy/culture, ECG, and baseline echocardiogram without delaying treatment. Treat confirmed or strongly suspected KD with IVIG 2 g/kg over 8-12 hours plus aspirin 5 mg/kg PO daily until cardiology review around 6 weeks. Add corticosteroid pathway for high CAA risk or clinician decision, and transfer if cardiac involvement or timely echo is unavailable.

children.label: Urticaria or anaphylaxis route
children.edgeLabel: Hives/angioedema with airway, breathing, circulation, or severe GI symptoms; or isolated migratory wheals after anaphylaxis is excluded; chronic hives >6 weeks

criteria.description: Use when hives, angioedema, or allergy symptoms determine emergency treatment, antihistamine dosing, observation, or allergy follow-up.

criteria_options.label: Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline 1:1000 by weight band, repeat/escalate after 2 doses; observe >=4 hours after last adrenaline or symptom onset
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH anaphylaxis guidance uses IM adrenaline 1:1000 volumes by weight band (0.1 mL under 7.5-10 kg, 0.15 mL at 15 kg, 0.2 mL at 20 kg, 0.3 mL at 30 kg, 0.4 mL at 40 kg, 0.5 mL above 50 kg), defines refractory anaphylaxis as persisting respiratory or cardiovascular symptoms despite 2 appropriate IM doses, and observes all children for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose or symptom onset.

criteria_options.label: Urticaria: acute hives use cetirizine age dosing; severe nonresponse prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg max 60 mg; chronic >6 weeks may increase cetirizine to 4x dose

children.action: If airway, breathing, circulation, collapse, hypotension, or severe persistent GI symptoms are present, remove allergen when possible and give IM adrenaline 1:1000 using the weight band chart; give adrenaline before asthma medicines when asthma coexists. Escalate after 2 appropriate IM doses or infusion need. Observe anaphylaxis at least 4 hours after last adrenaline or symptom onset. For isolated acute urticaria, avoid routine allergy tests, remove triggers, use cetirizine age dosing, consider one prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg dose max 60 mg only for severe antihistamine nonresponse, and arrange allergy review for persistent/systemic cases.

criteria.description: Use when rash morphology and exam point to cellulitis, erysipelas, impetigo, purulent cellulitis, or cutaneous abscess and severity changes drainage, antibiotics, admission, or culture.

children.action: Measure erythema/abscess diameter and assess systemic illness, hemodynamics, immunocompromise, necrotizing concern, adherence, and outpatient failure. Drain abscess and send culture/MRSA-SA PCR at I&D. Use 5 days of oral therapy for uncomplicated outpatient infection when antibiotics are indicated: TMP-SMX 8 mg/kg TMP q12h max 320 mg, doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg q12h max 100 mg, clindamycin 10 mg/kg q8h max 450 mg if susceptible, or cephalexin 50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h max 500 mg when MRSA risk is low. Admit severe/complicated infection for IV cefazolin or vancomycin by MRSA/immunocompromise/ill appearance and ID/surgery input.

children.label: Rash special-population review
children.edgeLabel: Age <6 months, newborn petechiae, immunocompromise, incomplete immunization, pregnancy, drug/mucosal severe rash, periorbital/orbital site, chronic urticaria >6 weeks, urticarial vasculitis lesions >48 hours, or live vaccines after IVIG

criteria.description: Use when age, immune status, pregnancy, medication reaction, anatomic site, chronicity, or treatment contraindication changes pediatric rash care.

children.action: Get senior pediatrics, dermatology, infectious diseases, allergy, cardiology, ophthalmology, surgery, safeguarding, or obstetric advice. Resolve ceftriaxone avoidance in neonates <41 weeks or calcium/TPN exposure, pregnancy-safe medicines, immunocompromised host coverage, orbital/periorbital and difficult abscess drainage, chronic urticaria or vasculitis workup, suspected severe drug eruption, and live-vaccine delay for 11 months after IVIG.
children.review_needed_reason: Specialist review is required when pediatric rash management depends on neonatal antibiotic safety, immunocompromise, pregnancy, severe drug eruption, orbital/periorbital disease, difficult drainage, Kawasaki cardiology, chronic urticaria, or post-IVIG vaccination timing.

criteria.description: Use when rash morphology, distribution, systemic findings, exposure, trauma, or labs make a non-primary rash diagnosis more likely than meningococcal disease, KD, anaphylaxis, or SSTI.

children.action: Name the competing diagnosis and start disease-specific care: mechanical petechiae only after cough/vomiting and above the nipple line, IgA vasculitis with purpura plus abdominal/joint/urine findings, ITP/leukemia/DIC/HUS with bleeding or abnormal FBE/film, erythema multiforme target lesions, measles or scarlet fever features, MIS-C/PIMS-TS, toxic shock, eczema/contact dermatitis, or safeguarding concern. Discharge benign rash only after serious causes are considered unlikely and follow-up is reliable.
children.review_needed_reason: Clinician review should confirm the named alternate rash diagnosis, serious-cause exclusions, and follow-up ownership before closure.

criteria_options.criteria_text: Primary Kawasaki therapy is IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion over 8-12 hours and aspirin 5 mg/kg orally daily until follow-up cardiology review around 6 weeks post onset.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment

criteria_options.label: Non-blanching rash risk: fever plus ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, CRT >=3 seconds, neck stiffness, shock, altered mental status, or difficulty mobilizing
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE flags meningococcal disease when fever and non-blanching rash occur, especially with ill appearance, purpura >2 mm, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, or neck stiffness; RCH treats unwell children with petechiae/purpura as urgent and notes shock, altered conscious state, abnormal vitals, and limb tenderness or difficulty mobilizing as unwell features.
criteria_options.source_section: Meningococcal disease signs and petechiae/purpura assessment

criteria_options.label: Unwell petechiae/purpura antibiotics: ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily max 4 g, avoid neonates <41 weeks; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV by neonatal age interval
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH petechiae/purpura antimicrobial notes list ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg IV daily (maximum 4 g) and advise avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates <41 weeks gestation, especially if jaundiced or receiving calcium-containing solutions; cefotaxime 50 mg/kg IV is dosed every 12 hours in week 1, every 6-8 hours in weeks 2-4, and every 6 hours after week 4.
criteria_options.source_section: Management antibiotic dosing footnote

criteria_options.label: Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline 1:1000 by weight band, repeat/escalate after 2 doses; observe >=4 hours after last adrenaline or symptom onset
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH anaphylaxis guidance uses IM adrenaline 1:1000 volumes by weight band (0.1 mL under 7.5-10 kg, 0.15 mL at 15 kg, 0.2 mL at 20 kg, 0.3 mL at 30 kg, 0.4 mL at 40 kg, 0.5 mL above 50 kg), defines refractory anaphylaxis as persisting respiratory or cardiovascular symptoms despite 2 appropriate IM doses, and observes all children for at least 4 hours after the last adrenaline dose or symptom onset.
criteria_options.source_section: Adrenaline dose chart, observation and admission, urticaria anaphylaxis screen

criteria_options.label: Urticaria: acute hives use cetirizine age dosing; severe nonresponse prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg max 60 mg; chronic >6 weeks may increase cetirizine to 4x dose
criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH urticaria guidance states acute urticaria usually needs no investigations, uses non-sedating antihistamines such as cetirizine with age-based dosing, may use one oral prednisolone dose 0.5-1 mg/kg (max 60 mg) in severe cases not responding to antihistamines, and chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) can increase cetirizine up to 4 times the recommended daily dose with specialist advice if symptoms persist.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment, chronic urticaria, consultation and discharge

criteria_options.label: Pediatric SSTI: abscess/cellulitis <5 cm mild, >5 cm or systemic symptoms moderate; oral antibiotics 5 days; TMP-SMX, doxycycline, clindamycin, cephalexin doses
criteria_options.criteria_text: Johns Hopkins All Children's SSTI pathway classifies suppurative abscess/cellulitis as mild when total cellulitis plus abscess is <5 cm with no systemic symptoms, moderate when >5 cm and/or systemic symptoms, and severe/complicated with hemodynamic instability, toxic appearance, immunocompromise, necrotizing concern, poor adherence/social concern, failed I&D, or failed outpatient therapy. It recommends I&D as primary abscess treatment, oral antibiotics for 5 days, and lists TMP-SMX 8 mg/kg TMP component q12h max 320 mg TMP, doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg q12h max 100 mg, clindamycin 10 mg/kg q8h max 450 mg, or cephalexin 50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h max 500 mg.

children.action: Repeat vitals, mental status, perfusion/CRT, rash blanching and spread, mucosal/eye findings, airway/breathing, abdominal/joint/urine symptoms, skin infection border/drainage, medication timing/doses, culture/FBE-film/coag/CRP/ESR/urine/echo status, treatment response, adverse effects, and caregiver access before closing the pathway.

children.label: Rash worsening or treatment failure

criteria.description: Escalate pediatric rash care when reassessment contradicts the current lower-risk branch or treatment response is inadequate.

children.action: Narrow the plan only when the cited branch is objectively improving: benign petechiae explanation or negative senior review, anaphylaxis stable for at least 4 hours, KD afebrile/well for at least 36 hours with aspirin and echo follow-up, SSTI improving after expected early 24-hour worsening window, or drained abscess without systemic illness. Document what changed and who owns pending results.

criteria_options.criteria_text: RCH urticaria guidance states acute urticaria usually needs no investigations, uses non-sedating antihistamines such as cetirizine with age-based dosing, may use one oral prednisolone dose 0.5-1 mg/kg (max 60 mg) in severe cases not responding to antihistamines, and chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) can increase cetirizine up to 4 times the recommended daily dose with specialist advice if symptoms persist.
criteria_options.source_section: Treatment, chronic urticaria, consultation and discharge

criteria_options.label: Pediatric SSTI: abscess/cellulitis <5 cm mild, >5 cm or systemic symptoms moderate; oral antibiotics 5 days; TMP-SMX, doxycycline, clindamycin, cephalexin doses
criteria_options.criteria_text: Johns Hopkins All Children's SSTI pathway classifies suppurative abscess/cellulitis as mild when total cellulitis plus abscess is <5 cm with no systemic symptoms, moderate when >5 cm and/or systemic symptoms, and severe/complicated with hemodynamic instability, toxic appearance, immunocompromise, necrotizing concern, poor adherence/social concern, failed I&D, or failed outpatient therapy. It recommends I&D as primary abscess treatment, oral antibiotics for 5 days, and lists TMP-SMX 8 mg/kg TMP component q12h max 320 mg TMP, doxycycline 2.2 mg/kg q12h max 100 mg, clindamycin 10 mg/kg q8h max 450 mg, or cephalexin 50 mg/kg/day divided q6-8h max 500 mg.
criteria_options.source_section: Non-suppurative and suppurative SSTI algorithms and antimicrobial therapy

children.action: Document diagnosis or working diagnosis, serious conditions considered, exact medicine doses, pending result owner, follow-up service and interval, and caregiver return precautions for becoming more unwell, fever recurrence, spreading/non-blanching rash, breathing trouble, swelling, vomiting/collapse, new bleeding/bruising, worsening pain/redness, dehydration, medication reaction, inability to take treatment, or missed follow-up.
